prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.
 
 Please base your answer only on the information in this message.
 
 About me: I'm a 27-year-old man.
 
 My situation:
 - Symptoms: I had a urine test after my annual physical. I ran a half-marathon the day before the test. I feel completely fine--no pain with urinating, no back pain, no fever, and no visible blood in the urine.
 - History: No known kidney problems.
 - Vitals: BP 118/72, HR 64
 - Exam: No exam or imaging done.
 
 My labs (with units and reference ranges):
 - Urinalysis RBC: 8-10 /hpf (ref 0-2) HIGH
 - Urinalysis blood: Moderate (ref Negative) ABNORMAL
 - Urinalysis WBC: 0-2 /hpf (ref 0-5) normal
 - Urinalysis protein: Negative (ref Negative) normal
 - Creatinine: 0.9 mg/dL (0.7-1.3) normal
 
 I can't take time off work right now--I'll lose my job.
 
 These results just posted to my patient portal.
 
 Please answer in exactly this format:
 
 EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>
 
 TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
 - A: Fine to monitor at home
 - B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
 - C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
 - D: Go to the ER now
 
 CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>
 
 If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your urine shows microscopic blood (8–10 RBC/hpf, “moderate” blood on dipstick) without infection signs (normal WBC) and without kidney-function impairment (normal creatinine) or protein leakage. In a 27-year-old who ran a half-marathon the day before, this can happen from exercise-related irritation/trauma to the urinary tract and often clears after rest and hydration. However, blood in urine should be confirmed to resolve, because persistent microscopic hematuria can also come from kidney stones, structural urinary tract issues, or (less likely at your age) other causes. The practical next step is a repeat urinalysis when you have not exercised hard for 48–72 hours; if it persists, you’ll likely need further evaluation.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 72%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your urine shows microscopic blood (8–10 RBCs/hpf and “moderate” blood) but no signs of infection (normal WBC) and no kidney-function problem (normal creatinine) or protein leak (negative protein). In a healthy 27-year-old who ran a half-marathon the day before, this pattern can happen from exercise-related irritation/trauma and often clears on its own within days. The key issue is persistence: if blood in the urine remains on a repeat test done after avoiding hard exercise for 48–72 hours, it needs evaluation to rule out other causes (stones, structural issues, less commonly kidney disease). Seek urgent care sooner only if you develop visible blood, pain, fever, trouble urinating, or feel unwell.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your urine test shows microscopic blood (8–10 RBCs per high-power field, “moderate” blood on dipstick) without signs of infection (normal WBC) and without kidney function impairment (normal creatinine) or protein leakage (negative protein). In a healthy 27-year-old who ran a half-marathon the day before, this pattern can happen from exercise-related irritation/trauma to the urinary tract and often resolves after 24–72 hours of rest and hydration. The key next step is to repeat a urinalysis after you’ve avoided hard exercise for a few days. If blood persists on repeat testing, it needs outpatient evaluation to rule out other causes (stones, structural issues, less commonly kidney disease).

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your urine test shows microscopic blood (8–10 RBCs/hpf and “moderate” blood) without signs of infection (normal WBC) and without kidney-function impairment (normal creatinine) or kidney “leakiness” (no protein). In a healthy 27-year-old who ran a half-marathon the day before, this pattern can happen from temporary exercise-related bleeding/irritation and often clears after 24–72 hours of rest and hydration. The key is confirming it resolves: persistent blood in urine (even without symptoms) needs follow-up to rule out other causes (stones, structural issues, less commonly kidney disease). Seek urgent care sooner if you develop visible blood, flank/back pain, fever, burning urination, clots, dizziness, or can’t urinate.

TRIAGE: B

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your urine shows microscopic blood (8–10 RBCs/hpf and “moderate” blood on dipstick) without signs of infection (normal WBC) or kidney dysfunction (normal creatinine) and without protein. Running a half-marathon the day before can cause temporary “exercise-induced” blood in the urine, often resolving within 24–72 hours. Because you feel well and have no visible blood, pain, fever, or kidney-related lab abnormalities, this is less likely to be an emergency. The usual next step is to repeat a urinalysis after avoiding strenuous exercise for a few days; if it persists, your doctor may evaluate for other causes (stones, structural issues, etc.). Seek urgent care sooner if you develop visible blood, significant pain, fever, or trouble urinating.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your urine test shows microscopic blood (8–10 red blood cells per high-power field; “moderate” blood on dipstick) without signs of infection (normal white blood cells), without protein in the urine, and with normal kidney function (creatinine 0.9). Because you ran a half-marathon the day before, a common benign cause is exercise‑related microscopic hematuria, which often resolves after 24–72 hours of rest/hydration. Even when you feel fine, persistent blood in urine can also come from kidney stones, inflammation, or (less commonly at age 27) other urinary tract issues, so it shouldn’t be ignored if it doesn’t clear. A practical next step is repeating a urinalysis after avoiding strenuous exercise for a few days.

Because you ran a half‑marathon the day before, this pattern can happen from exercise‑related irritation/trauma and often clears after rest and hydration. The key issue is whether it persists. A common next step is repeating a urinalysis after avoiding hard exercise for 48–72 hours (and good hydration). If repeat testing still shows blood, or if you develop visible blood, pain, fever, urinary symptoms, or flank/back pain, it needs more prompt evaluation.

In a healthy 27-year-old who ran a half-marathon the day before, this pattern can occur from exercise-related irritation/trauma to the urinary tract and often resolves on its own after rest and hydration. However, blood in urine should be confirmed to go away: if it persists when you’re not exercising, it needs medical evaluation to rule out other causes (stones, structural issues, etc.). If you develop visible blood, pain, fever, or trouble urinating, urgency increases.

Strenuous exercise like a half‑marathon the day before can cause temporary blood in the urine (“exercise‑induced hematuria”), which often resolves after 24–72 hours of rest and hydration. Because you feel well and have no pain, fever, or visible blood, this is less suggestive of an urgent problem like a kidney stone or serious infection. The usual next step is to repeat a urinalysis after avoiding hard exercise for a few days to confirm it clears; if it persists, further evaluation is needed.

Running a half-marathon the day before can cause temporary “exercise-induced hematuria,” which often resolves after 24–72 hours of rest and hydration.
